key_departures.impact: Three-time Pro Bowler and arguably the best center in the NFL. Ravens failed to retain him after not exercising his 5th-year option and not using the franchise tag. The loss creates a gaping hole at the most important interior line position. Corey Bullock (UDFA backup) is currently the in-house candidate, representing a massive step down in quality.

Baltimore's most versatile pass-catching TE. Followed new Giants HC John Harbaugh to New York. With Kolar also gone, only Mark Andrews (re-signed last fall) remains at TE. From a two-TE system to a one-TE depth chart overnight.

Premium blocking TE who complemented Andrews and Likely. His departure, combined with Likely's exit, strips Baltimore of their entire 2025 TE complement alongside Andrews. Need for TE depth and a complementary receiving TE now critical.

Trade-deadline acquisition who was productive (2.5 sacks, 17 tackles, 15 QB hits in 9 games). Departure was expected given the Ravens' pursuit of Maxx Crosby, with Mike Green expected to step into a starting role. However, Crosby's trade now fell through, leaving EDGE as a critical unaddressed need.

Starting safety who had been a consistent contributor. His departure, combined with Ar'Darius Washington leaving, strips Baltimore of both of their backup/depth safeties behind Kyle Hamilton and Malaki Starks. Team now lacks a viable #3 safety.
Returning from torn Achilles (played only 4 games in 2025). Depth/rotational safety. His departure contributes to a thin safety room behind Hamilton and Starks — Baltimore now needs a No. 3 safety.
First-team All-Pro punter in 2025, led NFL with 44.9 net yards per punt average. A genuine special teams asset lost. Need to find a replacement punter.

6x Pro Bowl fullback and 3x All-Pro who was the engine of Baltimore's ground game since 2017. Followed HC Harbaugh to New York. Moderate role loss — team ran full back-heavy schemes, but Harbaugh is gone anyway and new HC Minter may not deploy the position as heavily.
Special teams contributor with 18 tackles in limited role. Minimal roster impact.
Explosive RB with injury history. Ravens chose not to tender him with Justice Hill and Rasheen Ali as backups behind Derrick Henry. Minimal depth loss.

notable_failed_transaction.details: Ravens agreed on March 6 to send their 2026 (#14 overall) and 2027 first-round picks to Las Vegas Raiders for All-Pro EDGE Maxx Crosby. On March 11, the Raiders announced Baltimore backed out. Per The Athletic's Dianna Russini, Crosby failed his physical. Baltimore's medical staff had concerns about Crosby's knee surgery (full meniscus repair). Ravens retain both first-round picks. This dramatically alters Baltimore's draft and EDGE outlook — they now have two first-rounders back but no Crosby.
notable_failed_transaction.impact_on_needs: EDGE remains a Tier 1 critical need — arguably the most pressing need on the roster entering the 2026 draft. The failed trade also means the cap implications of Crosby's $30M cap hit no longer apply.

signings.analysis: Huntley re-signed as Lamar Jackson's backup on a 2yr/$5M base deal (up to $11M with incentives). His dual-threat ability mirrors Jackson's style and he went 2-0 as a starter in 2025. This was a value re-sign that locks in the backup QB position — QB is not a need. Effectively replaces Cooper Rush, who was a poor fit. Contract structure (depth-level AAV) correctly reflects his backup role.

contract.notes: Guaranteed money TBD at signing; $10M AAV ranks 11th among LGs per OTC. Guarantee structure not yet fully reported.

signings.analysis: Simpson returns to Baltimore (previously 2023 starter) on a 3yr/$30M deal, addressing the guard position with a known commodity. He started all 34 possible games over his last two seasons with the Jets. However, this signing only partially addresses IOL needs: (1) Simpson fills one guard spot (likely LG), but RG remains unsettled between Emery Jones Jr. and Andrew Vorhees. (2) CENTER is completely unaddressed — Linderbaum's departure creates a critical hole that Simpson does not solve. IOL remains a multi-faceted need at center specifically. PFF grades on Simpson were inconsistent in 2025 (54th out of 81 guards), and he is known for drawing penalties. Mid-tier starter deal — commits to him as the LG, but this is not a full IOL solution.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Dre'Mont Jones departed to New England. The Maxx Crosby trade — agreed to on March 6 and expected to solve this position — fell through after a failed physical on March 11. Baltimore now retains their 2026 and 2027 first-round picks but has NO EDGE acquisition to show for it. Only Mike Green and Tavius Robinson remain as in-house options. Baltimore ranked 30th in the NFL in sacks in 2025 (the first year since 1996 without an EDGE recording 4.5+ sacks). EDGE remains an absolute Tier 1 critical need — possibly the highest priority on the board entering the draft.

John Simpson partially addresses the guard need (LG). However, Tyler Linderbaum's departure to Las Vegas on a record $81M deal creates a gaping hole at CENTER that has not been addressed. Baltimore's current in-house option is Corey Bullock, a UDFA backup who has never started a regular-season game. IOL remains a Tier 1 need, now split across two sub-positions: (1) C — critical need, no viable starter in house; (2) RG — still unsettled competition between Emery Jones Jr. and Andrew Vorhees. The Simpson signing reduces the urgency at LG from critical to moderate, but the center hole keeps IOL as a top-tier draft need.

Both Isaiah Likely (3yr/$40M, NYG) and Charlie Kolar (3yr/$24.3M, LAC) departed in free agency. Mark Andrews remains (re-signed in December on 3yr/$39.2M), but Baltimore went from a 3-TE system to a 1-TE depth chart overnight. No TE has been signed to replace Likely or Kolar. Andrews is 30 and primarily a pass-catcher — the blocking TE role Kolar held is completely vacated. TE should escalate from Tier 2 to Tier 1 — this is a two-player void at a position-group level, not just a backup loss.

Baltimore lost both Alohi Gilman (3yr/$24.75M, KC) and Ar'Darius Washington (1yr, NYG) in free agency. Kyle Hamilton and Malaki Starks are locked in as the top two safeties and are excellent, but the depth behind them is now nearly nonexistent. Baltimore's new HC Jesse Minter favors nickel/sub-package defense requiring a third safety, and the team currently cannot run that scheme at full strength. Safety need escalates from Tier 3 to Tier 2 — a reliable No. 3 safety / nickel-versatile DB must be added either in free agency or the draft.

No movement at CB in free agency. Nate Wiggins is the emerging starter. Chidobe Awuzie is an unrestricted free agent still unsigned. Marlon Humphrey (aging, near 30) and a young/unproven room beyond Wiggins. CB need is unchanged — remains Tier 2 entering draft.

No additions or departures to IDL in confirmed free agency moves. Nnamdi Madubuike's status (potential return from injury) is a key swing factor — if he returns healthy, IDL improves substantially. However, no signed additions yet. IDL remains a Tier 2 need, unchanged.
No WR additions or losses confirmed. Wide receiver was a pre-FA need and no movement has occurred. Remains Tier 2.

NEW NEED CREATED. Tyler Linderbaum departed on a record 3yr/$81M deal to the Raiders. This is the most specific sub-position need created in this free agency window. Center was not a need before FA — it is now a critical Tier 1 need. Corey Bullock is the only in-house option and is not a reliable starter. Draft (Auburn's Connor Lew, Kansas State's Sam Hecht, Iowa's Logan Jones) or late free agency are the primary paths to a solution.

key_additions.headline: Veteran guard returns to Baltimore on 3-year deal, partially addressing offensive line need at LG
Dual-threat backup re-signed on value deal; went 2-0 as starter in 2025, locks in Jackson's insurance policy

key_losses.headline: 3x Pro Bowl center departs on record-setting deal, creating critical hole at Baltimore's most important interior line spot
25-year-old pass-catching TE follows HC Harbaugh to New York, leaving Andrews as Baltimore's only TE under contract
Highest-paid blocking TE in NFL departs; combined with Likely's exit, Baltimore loses its entire complementary TE room
Trade-deadline acquisition leaves via free agency; combined with failed Maxx Crosby trade, EDGE remains Baltimore's biggest unaddressed need
Starting safety departs to AFC rival; combined with Washington's departure, leaves thin depth behind Hamilton and Starks
6x Pro Bowl fullback and cornerstone of Baltimore's run game for 9 seasons follows HC Harbaugh to New York
2025 first-team All-Pro punter (50.1 yds avg, 44.9 net) follows Harbaugh to New York, leaves Ravens with unresolved punter vacancy
Versatile depth safety follows Harbaugh to New York, further depleting Baltimore's safety depth

notable_failed_transaction.headline: Blockbuster Crosby trade agreed March 6 (2 first-round picks), voided March 11 after failed physical — Ravens retain 2026 and 2027 first-round picks, EDGE still a critical need

draft_capital_notes: Ravens retain their 2026 first-round pick (#14 overall) and their 2027 first-round pick after the Maxx Crosby trade was voided. With no first-round pick previously spent, Baltimore enters the draft with strong capital and the #14 pick to potentially address EDGE, IOL (C), or TE at the top of the board.
